title: Code Sandbox
subtitle: Write, run, and experiment with Factory pattern code. Tests validate your implementation.

templates.label: Simple Factory
templates.description: Build a NotificationFactory that creates Email, SMS, and Push notifications. Run the code to see it work, then try the experiments.

testCases.name: Types are correct
testCases.description: getType() returns the right string for each

experiments.title: Add WhatsApp
experiments.prompt: Add a WhatsAppNotification class and register it in the Factory. How many files would you change?
experiments.hint: Create class WhatsAppNotification implements Notification, add a case in the switch, and test with NotificationFactory.create("whatsapp"). Answer: just 1 file (the Factory). That's the point!

experiments.title: Test Unknown Type
experiments.prompt: What happens if you call NotificationFactory.create("telegram")? Add a try-catch to handle it gracefully.
experiments.hint: Wrap in try-catch(IllegalArgumentException e). The Factory should fail fast with a clear message telling you what types ARE available.

experiments.title: Convert to Registry
experiments.prompt: Replace the switch statement with a Map<String, Supplier<Notification>>. Zero switch, zero if-else.
experiments.hint: Use Map<String, Supplier<Notification>> registry = new HashMap<>(); and register() method. create() becomes: registry.get(type).get();

templates.label: Registry Pattern
templates.description: Zero switch statements. A Map<String, Supplier> handles creation. Register types dynamically — even at runtime.

testCases.name: Runtime registration
testCases.description: WhatsApp added at runtime without modifying Factory

experiments.title: Plugin System
experiments.prompt: Imagine each Notification type is a JAR plugin. How would plugins register themselves? (Think: static initializer blocks)
experiments.hint: Each plugin JAR has a class with: static { NotificationFactory.register("mytype", MyNotification::new); } — Just adding the JAR to classpath registers it. Zero code changes. This is how JDBC drivers work!

experiments.title: Prevent Override
experiments.prompt: What if someone calls register('email', MaliciousNotification::new)? Add protection against overwriting existing registrations.
experiments.hint: Add: if (registry.containsKey(type)) throw new IllegalStateException("Type '" + type + "' already registered"); Or use registry.putIfAbsent().

templates.label: Abstract Factory
templates.description: Create families of related objects. Regional compliance: India needs GST + ₹ + Hindi receipt. US needs Sales Tax + $ + English receipt. The family must vary TOGETHER.

experiments.title: Add EU Region
experiments.prompt: Add EUComplianceFactory with 20% VAT, Euro formatter (€), and EU receipt with VAT number. How many existing classes change?
experiments.hint: Create 3 new concrete products (VATCalculator, EuroFormatter, EUReceiptGenerator) + 1 new factory (EUComplianceFactory). ZERO existing classes change. That's Abstract Factory's superpower.

experiments.title: Mix Families (Bug!)
experiments.prompt: What happens if you accidentally use GSTCalculator with DollarFormatter? Why is this dangerous?
experiments.hint: You'd show GST tax in dollars — legally wrong! Abstract Factory PREVENTS this by only creating matching families. You can't get an India tax calculator from a US factory.

experiments.title: Factory of Factories
experiments.prompt: Add a static method: RegionalComplianceFactory getFactory(String region) that returns the right factory based on 'india' or 'us'.
experiments.hint: Use a Map<String, Supplier<RegionalComplianceFactory>> — yes, it's a Factory for Factories! This is common in real systems. Spring does this with @Profile.
